5.3

a.

repair = read.csv('/Users/kurtdecker/Downloads/School/Stats/Regression:Time Series/Data/Expanded Computer repair.csv')

repair_model = lm(Minutes ~ Units, data = repair)
summary(repair_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Minutes ~ Units, data = repair)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -31.603 -14.801  -0.045  17.335  29.092 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  37.2127     7.9853    4.66  0.00012 ***
## Units         9.9695     0.7218   13.81 2.56e-12 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 18.75 on 22 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8966, Adjusted R-squared:  0.8919 
## F-statistic: 190.7 on 1 and 22 DF,  p-value: 2.556e-12
plot(repair$Units, repair$Minutes)
abline(lm(Minutes ~ Units, data = repair), col = "darkgreen")

  1. Checking assumptions
#checking linearity
plot(repair_model$fitted.values, rstandard(repair_model),
     xlab = "Fitted Values",
     ylab = "Model Residuals",
     main = "Standardized Residuals vs Fitted Values")
abline(h = 0, col = "darkgreen") #there is not a random cloud of points on the residuals vs fitted. So the assumption of linearity is violated. This could also be a sign of non-equal variance but there isn't a funnel

# checking normality
library(ggpubr)
## Loading required package: ggplot2
ggqqplot(rstandard(repair_model))

plot(density(rstandard(repair_model)))

#there doesn't seem to be a violation of normality. The density plot of the residuals is bell shaped so that supports the assumption of normality.

5.7

cigarette = read.csv('/Users/kurtdecker/Downloads/School/Stats/Regression:Time Series/Data/Cigarette Consumption.csv')

round(cor(cigarette[,-1]), 3)
##           Age     HS Income  Black Female  Price  Sales
## Age     1.000 -0.099  0.257 -0.040  0.553  0.248  0.227
## HS     -0.099  1.000  0.534 -0.502 -0.417  0.057  0.067
## Income  0.257  0.534  1.000  0.017 -0.069  0.215  0.326
## Black  -0.040 -0.502  0.017  1.000  0.451 -0.148  0.190
## Female  0.553 -0.417 -0.069  0.451  1.000  0.022  0.146
## Price   0.248  0.057  0.215 -0.148  0.022  1.000 -0.301
## Sales   0.227  0.067  0.326  0.190  0.146 -0.301  1.000
pairs(cigarette[,-1], upper.panel = NULL)

smoke_model = lm(Sales ~ Price + Female + Black + Income + HS + Age, data = cigarette)
summary(smoke_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Sales ~ Price + Female + Black + Income + HS + Age, 
##     data = cigarette)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -48.398 -12.388  -5.367   6.270 133.213 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
## (Intercept) 103.34485  245.60719   0.421  0.67597   
## Price        -3.25492    1.03141  -3.156  0.00289 **
## Female       -1.05286    5.56101  -0.189  0.85071   
## Black         0.35754    0.48722   0.734  0.46695   
## Income        0.01895    0.01022   1.855  0.07036 . 
## HS           -0.06159    0.81468  -0.076  0.94008   
## Age           4.52045    3.21977   1.404  0.16735   
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 28.17 on 44 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.3208, Adjusted R-squared:  0.2282 
## F-statistic: 3.464 on 6 and 44 DF,  p-value: 0.006857
ggqqplot(rstandard(smoke_model))

5.8

exam = read.csv('/Users/kurtdecker/Downloads/School/Stats/Regression:Time Series/Data/Examination Data.csv')

#PR plots
model_1 = lm(F ~ P1, data = exam)
model_2 = lm(F ~ P2, data = exam)
model_3 = lm(F ~ P1 + P2, data = exam)

infl_1 = lm.influence(model_1)
infl_2 = lm.influence(model_2)
infl_3 = lm.influence(model_3)

SSE1 <- sum(model_1$residuals^2)
d1 <- model_1$residuals/sqrt(SSE1)

# Potential / Residual Plot
P1 <- infl_1$hat/(1-infl_1$hat)# Potential function
R1 <- (1+1)/(1-infl_1$hat)*(d1^2/(1-d1^2))   # Residual function

plot(R1,P1, main = "Potential vs. Residual Plot: model 1", pch = NA)
text(R1,P1, labels=seq_along(P1), cex = 0.5)

SSE2 <- sum(model_2$residuals^2)
d2 <- model_2$residuals/sqrt(SSE2)

# Potential / Residual Plot
P2 <- infl_2$hat/(1-infl_2$hat)# Potential function
R2 <- (1+1)/(1-infl_2$hat)*(d2^2/(1-d2^2))   # Residual function

plot(R2,P2, main = "Potential vs. Residual Plot: model 2", pch = NA)
text(R2,P2, labels=seq_along(P2), cex = 0.5)

SSE3 <- sum(model_3$residuals^2)
d3 <- model_3$residuals/sqrt(SSE3)

# Potential / Residual Plot
P3 <- infl_3$hat/(1-infl_3$hat)# Potential function
R3 <- (2+1)/(1-infl_3$hat)*(d3^2/(1-d3^2))   # Residual function

plot(R3,P3, main = "Potential vs. Residual Plot: model 3", pch = NA)
text(R3,P3, labels=seq_along(P3), cex = 0.5)
